High-Performance Computing on Lonestar6 Rich Herrington DRI, Research Computing Services Chapter 1 High-Performance Computing on Lonestar6 1.1 Overview This book is a practical introduction to high-performance computing using the Lonestar6 (LS6) system at the Texas Advanced Computing Center (TACC). Use it as: your course text your guided lab manual your workflow reference when you need to remember how to do something correctly on the cluster The goal is not only to help you run commands. The goal is to help you build good HPC habits: inspecting the environment, organizing files, requesting resources responsibly, interpreting output, and debugging from evidence rather than guesswork. Across the book you will move from: first login and basic HPC ideas to Linux fundamentals to SLURM job scheduling to compiling software to parallel computing to containers and reproducible workflows to a capstone project that brings the workflow together 1.2 What You Should Learn from This Book By the end of the book, you should be able to: explain the difference between login nodes and compute nodes navigate Linux confidently on a shared system manage files, permissions, and environment variables use modules to access software and compilers write, submit, monitor, and debug SLURM jobs compile and run C and C++ programs on an HPC system distinguish between serial, threaded, and distributed execution explain the roles of MPI, OpenMP, and containers in HPC workflows document an HPC workflow clearly enough that another user can reproduce it 1.3 What This Book Emphasizes Each chapter returns to the same core questions: What is this concept or tool for? What mental model should you use to understand it? What commands do you actually run? What usually goes wrong? How do you debug it when it fails? HPC rewards careful, inspectable, repeatable work. If you can run one successful job but cannot explain the environment, correct a resource request, or diagnose a failed run, you are not yet working independently. This book is meant to help you cross that gap. 1.4 How to Use the Chapters Most chapters include some or all of the following: overview mental model worked examples common mistakes lab exercises stretch goals summary points The repetition is deliberate. Strong HPC work usually follows a pattern: inspect the system make or edit a file run a small test scale up through the scheduler inspect output revise and rerun if needed This pattern matters because HPC work is rarely one big leap. It is usually a series of small, checkable steps. 1.5 How to Study from This Book A good way to use the book is: read the overview first pay attention to the mental model type and run the examples yourself complete the lab at the end of the chapter keep the appendix nearby as a quick reference return to earlier chapters when something fails later Do not treat the book as something to read once and set aside. Use it while you work. 1.6 Working Assumptions You do not need prior experience with: cluster computing batch scheduling software compilation parallel programming containers You do need patience, attention to detail, and a willingness to test things incrementally. On HPC systems, small mistakes are normal. What matters is learning how to find them and correct them. 1.7 A Practical Rule for the Whole Book Keep this rule visible as you work: Do setup on the login node. Do serious computation on compute nodes through the scheduler. That one distinction will help you avoid many of the most common beginner mistakes. 1.8 Looking Ahead The first chapter begins with the most basic question: what makes an HPC system different from an ordinary computer, and how should you orient yourself inside that environment?

Chapter 2 Introduction to High-Performance Computing on LS6 2.1 Overview High-performance computing, or HPC, is the practice of using powerful shared computing systems to solve problems that are too large, too slow, or too resource-intensive for an ordinary laptop or desktop computer. In this book, we use Lonestar6 (LS6) as the working example. The goal is not just to define HPC, but to help you begin using it productively. By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: describe what a cluster is explain the difference between login nodes and compute nodes identify common LS6 storage locations connect the basic HPC workflow to the commands you will use later 2.2 Why people use HPC There are three common reasons people move from a local machine to an HPC system. 2.2.1 1. The job is too slow on one machine A simulation that would take hours or days on a laptop may complete much faster on multiple cores or multiple nodes. 2.2.2 2. The job needs more memory Some workflows fail locally not because the CPU is too slow, but because the dataset, model, or matrix is too large for the memory available on the machine. 2.2.3 3. The work can be split into parts Many scientific and data-intensive problems can be broken into pieces and executed in parallel. HPC systems are designed for this style of work. 2.3 Mental model: a cluster is a collection of computers An HPC system is not one giant computer. It is better to think of it as a coordinated collection of computers working together. Important terms: node: one computer within the cluster core: one CPU execution unit on a node memory (RAM): memory available to processes or threads running on a node interconnect: the high-speed network that links nodes together This distinction matters because later chapters will ask you to request resources in terms of nodes, tasks, and cores. 2.4 The most important distinction: login node vs compute node Beginners often assume that once they log in to the cluster, they are already in the right place to run their real workload. That is not usually true. 2.4.1 Login nodes Login nodes are used for: editing files navigating directories loading modules compiling small programs moving data submitting jobs Login nodes are not the place for serious computation. 2.4.2 Compute nodes Compute nodes are used for: running scheduled jobs interactive compute sessions parallel programs training or simulation workloads large production tasks 2.4.3 Rule of thumb Do setup on the login node. Do computation on compute nodes. That single idea prevents many of the worst beginner mistakes. 2.5 A simple cluster workflow Here is the workflow you will repeat throughout the course: Laptop | | ssh v Login node | edit files, inspect directories, load modules | write job scripts | | sbatch v SLURM scheduler | v Compute node(s) | v Output files and results The scheduler in this workflow is SLURM, which will become the center of Chapters 3 and 4. 2.6 Connecting to LS6 A standard SSH connection looks like this: ssh your_username@ls6.tacc.utexas.edu After login, you may see a shell prompt like: [username@login1 ~]$ This prompt tells you several things at once: your username the machine name your current directory The prompt is not decoration. It gives you context. 2.7 Storage areas you will encounter Cluster workflows usually involve more than one storage location. 2.7.1 $HOME Your home directory is a personal, relatively safe place for configuration files, small scripts, and lightweight persistent material. 2.7.2 $WORK This is often a project-oriented area where you keep larger files, datasets, software builds, and materials that need to persist longer than temporary scratch storage. 2.7.3 $SCRATCH This is usually a high-performance temporary storage area. It is useful for active computation and temporary job data, but it should not be treated as permanent archival storage. 2.8 Why directories matter so much in HPC On a laptop, users often rely on clicking around to find files. In an HPC workflow, your current directory strongly influences: where your job looks for input where output files are written whether commands run on the files you intended That is why pwd and ls show up constantly in beginner workflows. 2.9 Minimal Linux toolkit for starting HPC work You do not need to master Linux before beginning. You do need a small set of reliable commands. pwd ls cd mkdir cp mv rm nano chmod +x These commands are enough to support the first real workflow in the course. 2.10 First exercise: create a workspace mkdir hpc_course cd hpc_course pwd ls This does four useful things: creates a directory for your course work moves you into that directory verifies where you are confirms what is inside it A surprising number of job failures come from skipping these checks. 2.11 First shell script Create a file: nano hello.sh Add: echo "Hello from LS6" Make it executable: chmod +x hello.sh Run it: ./hello.sh 2.12 What just happened? Even this small example teaches several important HPC habits. You: created a script file made the file executable ran a lightweight command safely on a login node confirmed that your shell environment is working This is appropriate login-node work because the task is tiny and brief. 2.13 Common beginner mistakes in Chapter 1 2.13.1 Running heavy work on the login node This is the most common conceptual mistake. Users often think, “I am logged in, so I can start the analysis.” In HPC, logging in gives you access to the environment, not ownership of shared computational resources. 2.13.2 Losing track of the current directory If you do not know where you are, you do not really know what your job will act on. 2.13.3 Forgetting execute permission A file can exist and still fail to run. ls -l and chmod +x are basic troubleshooting tools. 2.13.4 Treating storage areas as interchangeable Different storage areas serve different purposes. That matters for both performance and data safety. 2.14 Chapter lab Log in to LS6. Create a directory named hpc_course. Move into it and verify with pwd. Create hello.sh. Add one echo command. Make the script executable. Run it. Create a second script that prints your username, current directory, and date. Suggested commands: whoami pwd date 2.15 Reflection questions Why is a login node not the right place for serious computation? What is the difference between logging in and actually running a job? Why does your current directory matter before job submission? Which storage location would you trust for temporary high-volume output, and which would you treat more carefully? 2.16 Looking ahead In the next chapter, we slow down and build the Linux and environment skills that make later SLURM work easier. The theme of Chapter 2 is simple: good HPC habits begin with filesystem awareness and environment awareness.

Chapter 3 Linux Fundamentals for HPC 3.1 Overview In this chapter, you will build the Linux habits needed to work effectively on a shared HPC system. Many beginners think Linux is just a collection of commands to memorize. That is not the right way to approach it. On an HPC system, Linux is the environment in which you: find and organize your files inspect permissions prepare software environments launch and monitor work diagnose failure when something goes wrong So the real goal of this chapter is not just command memorization. The real goal is to help you become environment-aware. You should finish this chapter being able to answer questions like: Where am I? What files are here? Do I have permission to use this file? Is the command I need actually available? Is my software environment loaded correctly? Where did my output go? Am I using the right storage location? These questions come up constantly in real HPC work. 3.2 Why Linux matters in HPC On a laptop, you can often get away with clicking around until you find what you need. On an HPC system, that habit breaks down quickly. You are working in: a remote environment a shared filesystem a shell-based workflow a scheduler-managed system a software environment controlled by modules That means Linux skills are not optional background knowledge. They are part of the core workflow. If you lose track of your current directory, misunderstand permissions, or assume a command exists when it does not, your job may fail before the “real” scientific work even begins. This is why Linux fundamentals belong early in the book. Many later errors are really Linux errors in disguise. 3.3 Mental model: you are working in a shared environment Unlike a personal laptop, an HPC system is: multi-user policy-driven environment-sensitive designed for repeatable workflows That changes what “good habits” mean. On a personal machine, a messy directory or vague file naming may be annoying but manageable. On a shared cluster, messy habits lead to: lost files wrong paths broken scripts quota problems confusing job output workflows that are hard to reproduce later A strong habit on HPC is: inspect first, act second That one habit will prevent many avoidable mistakes. 3.4 The Linux filesystem A simplified picture looks like this: / ├── home/ │ └── your_username/ ├── work/ │ └── project_space/ ├── scratch/ │ └── temporary_files/ You do not need to memorize every system directory. You do need a clear working understanding of where your own files live and what each major storage area is for. 3.5 A practical storage model A useful beginner model is: /home or $HOME → small, stable, personal files /work or $WORK → active project files and larger working data /scratch or $SCRATCH → high-performance temporary data These names can vary somewhat by system, but the underlying ideas are common. 3.5.1 $HOME Use it for: shell configuration small scripts notes small code files personal settings Do not treat it as a dumping ground for large datasets, container images, or long-term accumulation of bulky outputs. 3.5.2 $WORK Use it for: active project directories larger inputs files you expect to revisit regularly project-oriented working material 3.5.3 $SCRATCH Use it for: temporary high-performance workflow data files created during active jobs large intermediate outputs that do not need permanent storage Scratch is useful because it is often better suited for active job I/O, but you should never forget that it is temporary by design. 3.6 Where am I? The first Linux question you should learn to ask is: pwd This prints your present working directory. This command is deceptively important. Many later failures are really just “I was in the wrong directory.” If you ever think: “Why can’t it find my file?” “Why did the output show up there?” “Why is my script failing to open the input?” start with pwd. 3.7 What is here? Use: ls For more detail: ls -l For hidden files too: ls -la A strong habit is to use ls -la more often than plain ls, because hidden files and permissions often matter in real workflows. 3.8 Reading ls -l Example output may look like: -rw-r--r-- 1 user group size date filename That one line tells you several useful things: file type permissions owner group size timestamp filename Instead of treating ls -l as clutter, learn to read it as diagnostic information. 3.9 File permissions Every file and directory has permissions for: owner group others Each of those can have: read (r) write (w) execute (x) 3.9.1 Visual reminder -rwxr-xr-- │││││││││ │││││││└── others │││││└──── group ││└──────── owner 3.10 Why permissions matter Permissions directly affect whether you can: run a script edit a file enter a directory read an input file share work safely In HPC, permissions matter because your jobs do not magically ignore filesystem rules. If the script cannot read a file, the scheduler will not fix that for you. 3.11 Common permission operations 3.11.1 Make a script executable chmod +x script.sh Use this when a file should behave like a runnable script rather than ordinary text. On Linux, the system does not assume that every text file containing commands is meant to be executed. The execute bit makes that intention explicit. This is one of the most common beginner fixes on HPC systems, especially when a job script or helper script exists, looks correct, but refuses to run. 3.11.2 Remove write permission chmod -w file.txt This is useful when you want to protect a file from accidental edits. For example, once an input file or reference script is finalized, removing write permission can reduce the chance that you accidentally change it during a later session. 3.11.3 Use numeric mode chmod 755 script.sh Numeric permission notation is compact and common in documentation, scripts, and examples. You do not need to love it immediately, but you do need to recognize what it is doing when you see it in real workflows. That means: user: rwx group: r-x others: r-x 3.12 A real permission example Suppose you create a shell script called run_analysis.sh and then type: ./run_analysis.sh If the system complains, do not immediately rewrite the script. Check: ls -l run_analysis.sh If the execute bit is missing, the fix is: chmod +x run_analysis.sh This is a good example of Linux debugging on HPC: do not guess inspect the file make the smallest needed correction rerun 3.13 Directories also have permissions Beginners often focus only on file permissions, but directories matter too. If you cannot enter a directory, list its contents, or write files into it, directory permissions may be the reason. So when something feels “missing,” do not think only about the file. Think about the directory path that leads to it. 3.14 Moving around the filesystem 3.14.1 Change directory cd my_directory This moves your shell into another directory. Because so many commands act on the current working directory, cd quietly changes the meaning of everything you do next. 3.14.2 Go up one level cd .. This moves you to the parent directory. It is especially useful when you realize you have gone too deep into a project tree and want to step back up without typing a full path. 3.14.3 Go to your home directory cd ~ This is a reliable way to reset yourself to a familiar location. When you feel disoriented, going home and checking pwd is often better than guessing where you are. 3.14.4 Return to the previous directory cd - This last one is especially useful when you are switching between two working locations. 3.15 Creating and organizing directories 3.15.1 Make a directory mkdir project1 This creates a new directory with the name you provide. On HPC systems, deliberate directory structure matters because it helps separate scripts, data, logs, and outputs before your workflow grows messy. 3.15.2 Make nested directories mkdir -p project1/data project1/output project1/scripts The -p option is helpful because it creates intermediate directories when needed and does not complain if some of them already exist. This makes it a good pattern for setting up a project structure quickly and repeatably. This is a good early habit because project organization matters. Strong directory structure makes debugging easier later. A clean small project structure may look like: project1/ ├── data/ ├── output/ ├── scripts/ └── notes/ Even small projects benefit from this kind of organization. 3.16 Copying, moving, and deleting 3.16.1 Copy a file cp file1.txt file2.txt This makes a duplicate of a file. It is useful when you want to preserve an original version before experimenting, editing, or moving the file elsewhere. 3.16.2 Copy a file into a directory cp file1.txt project1/data/ This keeps the original file and places a copy into the target directory. That is a good pattern when you are staging input files for a project without destroying the original location. 3.16.3 Move or rename a file mv oldname.txt newname.txt mv is used both for renaming and for moving files. That dual role is useful, but it also means you should pay attention to the destination because the same command can either tidy your naming or relocate the file entirely. 3.16.4 Remove a file rm file1.txt This permanently removes the file in the usual command-line sense. Because there is often no easy undo, it is worth checking the filename carefully before pressing Enter. 3.16.5 Remove a directory and its contents rm -r old_directory The -r option means recursive removal. That is powerful and potentially dangerous because it removes the directory and everything inside it, not just the top-level folder name. Be careful with rm. On many Linux systems, deleting a file this way does not send it to a recycle bin. That means inspection matters before deletion. 3.17 Viewing file contents 3.17.1 Print a short file cat file.txt Use cat when the file is short enough to read in one burst. It is convenient for quick checks, but not ideal for long output because it dumps everything to the screen immediately. 3.17.2 Read interactively less file.txt Use less when you want to scroll, search, and inspect a file without flooding the terminal. It is especially useful for job output, log files, and other text that may be longer than one screen. 3.17.3 Inspect the beginning head file.txt head is useful when you want a quick look at the first lines of a file. That often helps you confirm whether the file is the one you expected, whether it has a header, or whether the beginning of a log already shows a problem. 3.17.4 Inspect the end tail file.txt tail is useful because many programs report final status messages, recent progress, or fatal errors near the end of the file. It is often the fastest way to inspect the newest information in a log. These commands become extremely useful once you start reading job output files. A strong habit is: use head when you want the beginning quickly use tail when you want the last lines of a long output file use less when you want to scroll and search interactively 3.18 Searching for files Use: find . -name "file.txt" This command is one of the best ways to recover from a “Where did that file go?” problem. If output seems to be missing, find is often more useful than randomly changing directories and listing files. 3.19 Searching inside files Use: grep "error" file.txt This searches for lines containing a pattern. Example: grep "warning" sample.log This matters in HPC because output and error files can become long. You do not always want to read every line from top to bottom before looking for useful clues. 3.20 Pipes: connecting commands A pipe sends the output of one command into another command. Example: cat file.txt | grep "error" A better mental model is: one command produces information, the next command filters it This is valuable because Linux tools often work best in combination rather than isolation. You will use this pattern often for output inspection. 3.21 Environment variables Environment variables hold information about the current shell environment. Examples: echo $HOME echo $PATH echo $SHELL These variables are not just trivia. They tell you where your home directory is, how commands are discovered, and what shell is interpreting your session. When something behaves differently from what you expected, environment variables are often part of the explanation. This information helps define: where executables are found where your home directory is what shell you are using how your environment behaves 3.22 Why environment variables matter On HPC systems, many tools are activated through the environment rather than installed in one universal location. That means command behavior may depend on: loaded modules shell startup files path settings temporary environment choices So if something works in one shell but not another, the environment may be the reason. 3.23 Understanding PATH PATH tells Linux where to search for commands. Check it with: echo $PATH If a program is not in one of those directories, the shell may answer: command not found That does not always mean the program is absent from the system. It may mean the environment is not configured to find it. 3.24 Add something to PATH temporarily export PATH=$HOME/bin:$PATH This adds your personal bin directory to the front of the search path for the current shell session. That is useful when you write your own small scripts and want to run them by name. 3.25 Diagnosing a missing command Suppose you try: gcc --version and see: bash: gcc: command not found Do not guess wildly. Instead, work through a reasoning chain: Is the relevant module loaded? What does module list show? What does which gcc show? What is in PATH? This is much stronger than changing several unrelated settings at once. 3.26 Modules: software environments on HPC systems HPC systems often avoid putting all software into one global environment. Instead, they use modules. This matters because different projects may need: different compiler versions different MPI implementations different library stacks different dependency combinations Modules let you switch environments deliberately. 3.27 Basic module commands 3.27.1 Show available modules module avail This helps you answer the question, “What software environments could I activate right now?” It is often the first command to run when you know what tool you need but do not yet know its exact module name. 3.27.2 Load a module module load gcc This changes your current environment so that the requested software stack becomes active. Loading a module is not just “opening a program.” It often updates PATH, libraries, and related environment settings at the same time. 3.27.3 Show loaded modules module list This tells you what software environment is currently active. It is one of the best quick checks to run when a command behaves differently than you expected or when you need to document the environment you used. 3.27.4 Unload a module module unload gcc This removes the module from the current environment. It is useful when you want to switch toolchains, reduce environment clutter, or verify that a command really depends on that module being loaded. 3.28 Why modules matter so much On a personal machine, you may be used to software simply “being installed.” On HPC, software is often there but not active until you load the right module. That means module management is part of basic Linux practice on the cluster, not an advanced extra topic. 3.29 Example: using a compiler module load gcc which gcc gcc --version This pattern is excellent because it confirms: the module loaded the executable is now visible the version is what you expect 3.30 Disk usage and quotas 3.30.1 Check filesystem space df -h Use this when you want a broad view of available space on mounted filesystems. If a job starts failing while writing output, this command helps you check whether the problem might be lack of free space rather than a code bug. 3.30.2 Check directory sizes du -sh * Use this when you want to know which files or directories are consuming space in your current location. This is especially useful when a project directory becomes unexpectedly large and you need to find the main offenders quickly. These commands answer different questions. df -h shows overall filesystem space du -sh * shows how much space files and directories are using in your current location You need both perspectives. If a job starts failing when writing output, storage problems are a real possibility. 3.31 Why storage awareness matters Students often discover quota or storage trouble late, after: copying too much data writing many output files saving large containers in the wrong place leaving temporary data to accumulate A good habit is to inspect storage before it becomes an emergency. 3.32 Processes 3.32.1 Show your processes ps -u $USER This shows the processes currently running under your username. It is useful when you are trying to confirm whether something is still running, whether you accidentally launched a task on the login node, or whether a process you expected to finish is still active. 3.32.2 Kill a process kill <pid> Use this when you need to stop a process by its process ID. This is most useful for accidental or lightweight shell-side processes; for scheduled jobs, you will usually use SLURM commands such as scancel instead. 3.33 Repeating a command with watch Sometimes you do not want to run the same command over and over by hand. You want to see how something changes over time. That is exactly what watch is for. 3.33.1 Basic use watch squeue -u $USER This reruns the command every few seconds and refreshes the screen so you can watch the output change. This is especially useful when: you are waiting for a job to leave the queue you want to see a running job disappear when it finishes you want to monitor file growth, disk usage, or another changing value 3.33.2 Why watch is useful on HPC On an HPC system, many things change while you are working: a queued job becomes running a running job completes a log file grows disk usage increases during output generation Instead of typing the same command repeatedly, watch lets you see those changes live. 3.33.3 Choose a shorter interval watch -n 2 squeue -u $USER The -n 2 option tells watch to refresh every two seconds. That is often a good compromise: fast enough to be useful, but not so fast that the display becomes noisy. 3.33.4 Another good example watch -n 2 'ls -lh output/' This lets you see whether files in a directory are appearing or growing while a workflow is running. 3.33.5 A caution about watch watch is useful because it saves effort, but it is still repeatedly running a command. Use it deliberately rather than leaving many watch sessions running without reason. 3.34 Measuring runtime with time Another very useful command is time. Use it when you want a quick answer to a simple question: How long did that command actually take? 3.34.1 Basic use time ./hello This runs the command and reports timing information after it finishes. For small tests, this is often the simplest way to compare one version of a command or program against another. 3.34.2 Why time matters Even before you get to formal performance analysis, you should start building the habit of measuring instead of guessing. For example, time can help you compare: one program version versus another one compiler option versus another one input size versus another one small serial test before moving to a larger run 3.34.3 A practical example time grep "error" big_log.txt That example is small, but it reinforces the larger idea: Linux can help you measure behavior directly instead of relying on intuition. 3.34.4 A note for later chapters time is especially useful later when you begin comparing: serial and parallel programs optimized and non-optimized builds different workflow designs At this stage, you do not need a deep theory of performance. You just need the habit of checking real runtime when it matters. This is especially useful if you accidentally start something on the login node that should not keep running there. Later, SLURM job management becomes more important than plain process management, but this is still good Linux knowledge. 3.35 Working from inside R If you use R, remember that environment information can also be inspected from inside R. Sys.getenv("PATH") You can also run shell commands from R: system("echo $HOME") This is useful because R workflows on HPC still depend on the surrounding Linux environment. 3.36 A debugging mindset for Linux When something goes wrong, avoid random changes. Use a sequence. 3.36.1 1. Where am I? pwd This is the first grounding question. Before you do anything more complicated, make sure you know your current directory. 3.36.2 2. What files are here? ls -la This helps you verify whether the files you think are present are actually present, and whether any hidden files might matter. 3.36.3 3. Do I have the right permissions? ls -l script.sh If a script will not run, a file will not open, or a directory seems inaccessible, inspect permissions before changing anything else. 3.36.4 4. Is the command available? which gcc This checks whether the command can actually be found in the current environment. It is much better than assuming a program is installed just because you expect it to be. 3.36.5 5. Is the right environment loaded? module list This checks whether the software environment you intended to use is actually active. Many “mystery” failures are really just environment mismatches. 3.36.6 6. Am I out of space? df -h du -sh . These two checks help you decide whether the problem is global free space or local directory growth. Storage issues can look like program failures if you are not watching for them. That checklist is much more useful than reacting emotionally to an error message. 3.37 Mini case study: “command not found” Suppose you run: gcc --version and get: bash: gcc: command not found A good response is: check module list load the compiler if needed: module load gcc verify with which gcc rerun the command This is a small example, but it captures the larger lesson: Many Linux problems are environment problems. 3.38 Mini case study: wrong directory Suppose your job script expects input.csv, but the program cannot find it. Before rewriting the code, check: pwd ls find . -name "input.csv" Very often, the file exists but your current directory assumption is wrong. 3.39 Common mistakes forgetting chmod +x working in the wrong directory assuming software is available without checking modules misunderstanding PATH filling $HOME with large files leaving important temporary data only in scratch searching blindly instead of using find, head, tail, and grep 3.40 Lab 1: permissions and execution Create a file: touch test.sh Inspect it: ls -l test.sh Make it executable: chmod +x test.sh ls -l test.sh Write one sentence explaining what changed. 3.41 Lab 2: environment variables Run: echo $HOME echo $PATH export COURSE=HPC echo $COURSE Then open a new shell and test whether COURSE still exists. Explain why. 3.42 Lab 3: modules and command discovery Run: module avail module load gcc module list which gcc gcc --version Then explain the difference between: software existing on the system software being visible in your current environment 3.43 Lab 4: file search and output inspection Run: touch example.txt find . -name "example.txt" echo "warning: test" > sample.log grep "warning" sample.log head sample.log tail sample.log Then explain what each command helped you learn. 3.44 Lab 5: storage awareness Run: df -h du -sh ~ Then explain the difference between total filesystem usage and your own directory usage. 3.45 Lab 6: watch and time 3.45.1 Part 1: Watch a changing command Run: watch -n 2 squeue -u $USER If you do not currently have a job in the queue, try it anyway so you can see how the screen refreshes. Then explain what problem watch solves better than typing the same command repeatedly by hand. 3.45.2 Part 2: Measure a command with time Run: time ls -l Then run: time find . -name "example.txt" Write one or two sentences explaining what time tells you and why it is better to measure runtime than to guess. 3.46 Stretch goal create a custom bin/ directory add it to PATH place a script inside it run that script without typing the full path explain why the shell can now find it 3.47 Key takeaways Linux on HPC is about environment awareness, not just command memorization pwd, ls, and find help you stay oriented permissions affect whether files can be read, written, and executed PATH controls how commands are discovered modules control which software environment is active disk usage awareness prevents avoidable failures strong Linux debugging starts with inspection rather than guessing

Chapter 4 SLURM Job Scheduling on LS6 4.1 Overview On a shared HPC system, real computational work is normally submitted through a scheduler rather than run directly on the login node. On LS6, that scheduler is SLURM. That sentence is easy to memorize, but the real goal is deeper than memorization. You should understand: why a scheduler exists what a job request really means how a resource request affects queue behavior what evidence to inspect when a job does not behave the way you expected This chapter develops a practical workflow for beginner and intermediate batch jobs: write a job script request resources deliberately submit with sbatch monitor with squeue inspect finished jobs with sacct interpret output and error files diagnose common failures without guessing By the end of the chapter, you should be able to submit a real job, explain the meaning of your request, and work through the first layer of job debugging from evidence. 4.2 Mental model: SLURM is a resource manager, not a launch button A common beginner misunderstanding is to treat sbatch as if it were a special version of bash. It is not. When you run a shell script directly, the shell begins executing the script immediately in your current environment. When you submit with sbatch, you do something different: you hand SLURM a script you describe the resources needed for that script the scheduler decides when those resources are available the script later runs on one or more compute nodes So a SLURM job is not just “commands to run.” It is a contract between you and the scheduler: Here is the work I want to do, here are the resources I believe it needs, and here is how long I think it should take. That is why a SLURM script is both: a shell script, and a resource request document This is one of the biggest conceptual shifts in HPC. You are no longer asking only, “What command should I run?” You are also asking, “What is the correct shape of the request for this workload?” 4.3 Why clusters use schedulers A scheduler exists because HPC systems are shared. Without a scheduler: users would compete chaotically for access large jobs could crowd out smaller ones there would be no orderly queue usage tracking would be poor fairness would break down quickly With a scheduler: work is queued resource use is explicit policies can be enforced accounting is possible compute nodes remain organized as a shared service rather than a free-for-all This is one reason HPC feels different from a personal workstation. On your laptop, you mostly ask, “Can my machine run this?” On a cluster, you also ask, “What is the appropriate request for a shared system?” 4.4 The basic batch workflow Write job script ↓ Submit with sbatch ↓ Job enters queue ↓ SLURM decides when resources are available ↓ Job starts on compute node(s) ↓ Output and accounting records become available This small picture is worth slowing down for. Each step answers a different question. Write job script means you decide what should happen and what resources are needed. Submit with sbatch means you hand that plan to the scheduler. Job enters queue means the job is now waiting to be placed. SLURM decides when resources are available means the system is matching your request to real hardware and current demand. Job starts on compute node(s) means the job finally runs in the environment you asked for. Output and accounting records become available means you can inspect what happened instead of guessing. This workflow appears again and again in HPC practice. Later topics such as parallel computing, containers, compiled programs, Python workflows, and R workflows still pass through the same scheduler logic. 4.5 Login nodes versus compute nodes This distinction remains central. If you forget everything else in the chapter, keep this rule: Use the login node to prepare work. Use compute nodes to perform work. A login node is a front door into the cluster. It is meant for light, short, interactive work. 4.5.1 Login nodes are for: editing files reading documentation small test commands moving data compiling small programs submitting jobs 4.5.2 Compute nodes are for: real program execution long-running analyses memory-intensive work parallel workloads training or simulation runs If a task would monopolize CPU time, consume substantial memory, run for a long time, or scale across multiple cores, it belongs under SLURM. This matters both technically and socially. Technically, the compute nodes are where the real resources live. Socially, the login nodes are shared by many users, and heavy work there makes the system worse for everyone. 4.6 Your first SLURM script Create a file: nano job.slurm Add: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J hello_job #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:01:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Hello from compute node" hostname date pwd This script is intentionally simple. The goal is to make the scheduler behavior visible before the program logic becomes complicated. At this stage, a simple script is better than a “realistic” but confusing script. You want to see the scheduler, the compute node, and the output file clearly. 4.7 Reading the script line by line Before rushing to submission, it is worth learning how to read a job script calmly. A good job script is not mysterious. It is a small document that answers two questions: What resources are being requested? What commands should run once the job starts? The #SBATCH lines answer the first question. The ordinary shell commands answer the second. 4.7.1 Shebang #!/bin/bash This tells the system to execute the script using Bash. Even though this line looks familiar from ordinary shell scripting, it still matters here because a SLURM job script is still, at bottom, a shell script. 4.7.2 Job name #SBATCH -J hello_job This gives the job a readable name. Good names matter because later you may have many jobs in the queue or in the accounting records. A name like hello_job is fine for a first test. A name like analysis_run or omp_test_4threads becomes more useful later. 4.7.3 Nodes #SBATCH -N 1 This requests one node. At a beginner level, think of a node as one full machine in the cluster. For a simple serial or small shared-memory job, one node is often enough. 4.7.4 Tasks #SBATCH -n 1 This requests one task. For a simple serial job, that usually means one process slot. Later, for MPI jobs, this becomes more important because task count often maps to the number of MPI ranks. 4.7.5 Wall time #SBATCH -t 00:01:00 This tells SLURM the job should need at most one minute. This is not a casual estimate. It is part of scheduling. If you ask for far more time than needed, the job may wait longer than necessary. If you ask for too little time, the job may be terminated before it finishes. 4.7.6 Partition #SBATCH -p normal This requests the normal partition. A partition is a queue or class of resources. Different systems define partitions differently, but the key mental model is simple: A partition tells SLURM which pool of resources and policies your job belongs to. 4.8 Submit the job sbatch job.slurm Typical response: Submitted batch job 123456 That message does not mean your program has already run. It means SLURM has accepted the job into the scheduling system. This is the first beginner correction that really matters: sbatch submits. It does not promise immediate execution. A lot of early confusion disappears once you understand this. Submission success and program success are not the same thing. 4.9 Check the queue Use: squeue -u $USER Typical fields include: job ID partition job name state elapsed time number of nodes If the job starts quickly, you may barely see it. If not, squeue lets you observe the job state. This command matters because it answers a very practical question: Is my job still waiting, running, or gone? That is often the first thing to establish before you do any deeper debugging. 4.10 Common job states PD = pending R = running CG = completing CD = completed A pending job is not automatically a broken job. It may simply be waiting for resources, waiting behind other jobs, or waiting because your request is harder to place. If a job is pending, do not jump immediately to the conclusion that the script is wrong. First ask whether the scheduler has simply not placed it yet. 4.11 Output files are evidence When the batch job finishes, a file such as the following often appears: slurm-123456.out Inspect it with: cat slurm-123456.out or: less slurm-123456.out A strong habit is: do not guess what happened inspect the output file Your output file is often the fastest path from confusion to evidence. 4.12 What happened in the first example? When hello_job runs successfully, you have completed a real HPC workflow: wrote a scheduler-aware script requested resources submitted the request waited for SLURM to place the job ran on a compute node captured output in a file That may seem small, but it is the foundation for almost everything else in the book. 4.13 Resource requests: what you are really asking for A job request is best understood as a statement of workload shape. Questions you should learn to ask: Is this a serial job or a parallel job? Does it need one core or many? Does it need one node or several nodes? Is it short or long? Does it require a special software environment? Is the request realistic or inflated? These questions matter because scheduler performance depends on matching the request to the workload. 4.13.1 Too small a request can cause: slow performance thread or process mismatch jobs that terminate early poor behavior once the code actually scales up A request that is too small is not necessarily “efficient.” Sometimes it is simply wrong. 4.13.2 Too large a request can cause: unnecessary queue delay wasted allocation poor cluster citizenship confusion when the code uses only a fraction of what was requested Responsible HPC practice is not “ask for everything.” It is “ask for what the workload actually needs.” 4.14 A better beginner script: make the environment visible A useful early script is one that prints context: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J context_job #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:02:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Job ID: $SLURM_JOB_ID" echo "Job name: $SLURM_JOB_NAME" echo "Running on host:" hostname echo "Working directory:" pwd echo "Date:" date This helps you see that jobs run in a real environment with real metadata. It also makes debugging easier because you are recording context rather than assuming it. 4.15 The “sleep” demonstration To observe the queue and running state more clearly, use a job that lasts long enough to inspect. #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J sleep_demo #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:03:00 #SBATCH -p normal echo "Starting" hostname date sleep 30 echo "Finishing" date Submit it: sbatch sleep_demo.slurm Then watch: squeue -u $USER This is a small but powerful exercise because it makes the scheduler visible. 4.16 Why queue time can change You may wonder why one job starts immediately while another waits. The answer is usually some combination of: current system load partition policies how many resources were requested wall time requested job priority the shape of the free resources This is a good place to build maturity. Queue time is usually not arbitrary. It is the consequence of your request meeting a real shared system. 4.17 Output and error handling By default, many simple jobs write output to a file such as: slurm-123456.out For more deliberate control, you can specify output files directly: #SBATCH -o job_%j.out #SBATCH -e job_%j.err where %j expands to the job ID. This helps separate standard output from standard error and makes debugging cleaner. Example: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J split_output #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:02:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o split_%j.out #SBATCH -e split_%j.err echo "Standard output message" ls does_not_exist This is useful because the output and the error now go to different places. When you inspect them, you can tell the difference between: ordinary progress messages failure messages that need attention 4.18 A realistic serial job pattern Here is a more representative pattern for a serial program: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J serial_python #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:10:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o serial_%j.out #SBATCH -e serial_%j.err module load python python analysis.py This script has all the basic parts of real work: explicit resources explicit environment explicit program launch explicit output handling The more your scripts look like this, the easier they become to understand and reuse later. 4.19 A realistic compile-and-run pattern Sometimes the job script is used only to run an already-built executable: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J c_run #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o c_run_%j.out #SBATCH -e c_run_%j.err ./hello The separation matters: build small programs on the login node execute real workloads through SLURM Keeping those roles distinct will make later chapters easier to understand. 4.20 Common beginner mistakes These mistakes happen often enough that you should expect to see some of them in your own early work. The goal is not to avoid every mistake forever. The goal is to recognize them quickly and diagnose them calmly. 4.20.1 Mistake 1: Assuming sbatch ran the code right away Correction: sbatch submits to the scheduler. The code may run later. 4.20.2 Mistake 2: Running heavy work directly on the login node Correction: login nodes are for setup and lightweight tasks. 4.20.3 Mistake 3: Requesting many tasks for a serial program Correction: more requested resources do not help if the code uses only one process. 4.20.4 Mistake 4: Asking for far too much wall time Correction: inflated requests can delay scheduling and misrepresent workload needs. 4.20.5 Mistake 5: Ignoring output files Correction: output and error files are usually the first evidence to inspect. 4.20.6 Mistake 6: Submitting from the wrong directory Correction: confirm location with pwd and ls before submission. 4.21 A debugging checklist for failed jobs When a job behaves unexpectedly, work through the following checklist in order. This matters because debugging is much easier when you move from simple evidence to deeper interpretation instead of jumping randomly between ideas. 4.21.1 1. Did the job submit? Use: squeue -u $USER If the job is there, it is either queued or running. If it is not there, the job may have finished already or failed quickly. 4.21.2 2. What does accounting say? Use: sacct or: sacct -j 123456 This helps confirm whether the job completed, failed, or was cancelled. It is especially useful when the queue is empty and you are trying to figure out what happened after the fact. 4.21.3 3. What do the output and error files say? Use: cat slurm-123456.out or your custom output and error filenames. Do not skip this step. The output file often contains the most direct explanation of what went wrong. 4.21.4 4. Was the intended program or file actually present? Use: pwd ls This checks your working directory assumptions. A surprising number of job failures are really path failures. 4.21.5 5. Was the required software environment loaded? If your script assumes Python, GCC, MPI, or another toolchain, verify that the appropriate module commands are present. The scheduler does not automatically guess the environment you intended. 4.21.6 6. Did the resource request match the program model? A serial program with four tasks or an OpenMP program without thread settings may behave badly or misleadingly. If the request and the code do not match, the job may still run, but it may not run in the way you think it does. 4.22 Failure case study 1: misspelled command Suppose the script contains: pythno analysis.py The job may submit successfully, but the output or error file will show that the command is not found. The key lesson is: A job can fail even when submission succeeds. SLURM accepted the job request. The program inside the job then failed. 4.23 Failure case study 2: missing input file Suppose the script expects input.txt, but the file is not in the working directory: python analysis.py input.txt If input.txt is missing, the program may fail immediately. The fix is usually one of the following: submit from the correct directory use a correct path copy the needed input file first Again, pwd and ls help. 4.24 Failure case study 3: request does not match workload Suppose the script requests four tasks: #SBATCH -n 4 but runs a plain serial script: python analysis.py The code itself may still run, but the request does not reflect the workload. This wastes resources and creates confusion. A good HPC user learns that job design is part of computational discipline. 4.25 Interactive jobs versus batch jobs Although this chapter emphasizes batch submission, it is still useful to understand the difference conceptually. Both interactive and batch jobs run on compute resources. The difference is mostly in how you interact with them. 4.25.1 Batch jobs good for repeatable workflows good for production runs good for recorded output good for longer jobs Batch jobs are the default model for most serious work because they encourage planning, documentation, and repeatability. 4.25.2 Interactive jobs good for exploration good for debugging good for short tests in a compute environment Interactive jobs are helpful when you need to test behavior live, but they are not a substitute for a well-designed batch workflow. Learn batch first because it encourages deliberate, documented practice. 4.26 Best practices for SLURM scripts keep scripts readable name jobs clearly request realistic resources separate output and error when useful record environment details for debugging keep useful scripts rather than rewriting from memory each time treat scripts as workflow documents If you build this habit early, later chapters become easier because you are not inventing your workflow from scratch each time. 4.27 Lab 1: your first real job Create job.slurm Add the minimal hello example Submit with sbatch Monitor with squeue -u $USER Inspect the output file Identify the hostname and explain why it differs from the login node 4.28 Lab 2: observe a running job Create a script with sleep 30 Submit it Monitor the job while it is running Record the job state you observe Inspect output after completion 4.29 Lab 3: separate standard output and standard error Create a script with -o and -e Print one normal message Intentionally run a bad command Inspect the .out and .err files separately Explain the difference between them 4.30 Lab 4: diagnose a broken job Break one script intentionally by doing one of the following: misspell a command refer to a missing file omit a needed module load Then diagnose using: squeue sacct output and error files pwd and ls 4.31 Stretch goal Modify a job script so that it: prints job metadata writes separate output and error files loads a required module runs a short program records start and end times Then explain each line in plain English. 4.32 Key takeaways SLURM is a scheduler and resource manager, not a direct execution command a job script is both a shell script and a resource request sbatch submits work to the queue squeue shows queued and running work sacct helps interpret finished jobs output and error files are central debugging evidence thoughtful resource requests are part of responsible HPC practice

Chapter 5 Modules, Compilers, and Your First C Program 5.1 Overview In this chapter, we move from running shell commands and submitting SLURM scripts to building executable software from source code. This matters because much HPC work depends on software that is: written by researchers customized for a lab or project compiled against particular libraries sensitive to the compiler and environment used to build it That means compilation is not an optional side topic. It is part of the practical workflow of scientific computing. By the end of this chapter, you should be able to: explain what compilation does distinguish source files from executables load a compiler module verify which compiler is active compile a small C program with gcc explain the difference between compiling and linking at a basic level interpret common build errors prepare compiled code for execution through SLURM 5.2 Mental model: source code becomes an executable A compiled workflow looks like this: source code (.c) ↓ compiler ↓ object code and linking ↓ executable binary ↓ run on LS6 At a beginner level, this picture is enough to build the right intuition: the source file is written for humans the executable is written for the machine the compiler translates the build process may involve more than one file One of the most useful early realizations is that editing source code does not automatically change the executable. If you edit the source, you must rebuild the program. 5.3 Why compilation matters on clusters Compilation matters in HPC because: software is not always preinstalled research code may need to be built from source compiler and library versions must often match toolchains affect performance and reproducibility different module environments can produce different behavior On a laptop, beginners often get used to one built-in environment. On a cluster, you should think more deliberately: What compiler am I using? What libraries is it tied to? What environment is active right now? Those are normal questions in HPC. 5.4 Modules come before compilers Before compiling, inspect the available software environment: module avail Then load a compiler environment: module load gcc module list This reinforces a key course theme: On an HPC system, software is usually exposed through the environment rather than assumed to be globally fixed forever. 5.5 Verify the compiler instead of guessing Use: which gcc gcc --version This gives you evidence for: whether gcc is available where the executable is located which version is active That matters because troubleshooting gets much easier when you know exactly which tool you are using. 5.6 Your first C program Create a file named hello.c: nano hello.c Add: #include <stdio.h> int main() { printf("Hello from C on LS6
"); return 0; } This program is intentionally tiny. The point is not complexity. The point is making the compile-and-run pipeline visible. 5.7 Compile the program gcc hello.c -o hello Interpretation: hello.c is the source file -o hello names the output executable hello 5.8 Run the executable ./hello Expected output: Hello from C on LS6 5.9 Inspect the directory Use: ls -l You should see at least: hello.c hello This is an important conceptual moment. The source file and executable are different artifacts with different purposes. 5.10 What just happened? You: wrote human-readable source code invoked a compiler produced machine-executable output ran the resulting program This is the basic compiled workflow that underlies far more complex HPC software. 5.11 Compile versus link: a useful beginner distinction You will often hear the word “compile” used to describe the entire build process. In casual conversation that is common, but it helps to know that there are two ideas present: 5.11.1 Compilation Turning source code into lower-level object code. 5.11.2 Linking Combining object code and libraries into a final executable. At first, these steps are often hidden behind a single command like: gcc hello.c -o hello But conceptually, the compiler is doing more than one job on your behalf. Why does this matter? Because some errors are compile-time errors and others are link-time errors. 5.12 Compile-time errors These happen when the compiler cannot interpret the source correctly. Example: #include <stdio.h> int main() { prinft("Hello
"); return 0; } Notice the typo: prinft instead of printf. If you compile: gcc hello.c -o hello the compiler will report a problem. A compile-time error often points to: a typo invalid syntax missing punctuation misuse of the language 5.13 Link-time errors Link-time errors happen when source files may compile individually, but the final program cannot be assembled correctly. A common beginner example involves a missing function definition. Suppose main.c calls a function add, but the file containing add is not included in the build command. Then the compiler may accept main.c, but the linker will complain that the referenced symbol cannot be resolved. This distinction helps you read build messages more intelligently: syntax problems often mean compilation trouble undefined reference problems often mean linking trouble 5.14 A simple multiple-file example Imagine the following files: 5.14.1 main.c #include <stdio.h> #include "helper.h" int main() { printf("%d
", add(2, 3)); return 0; } 5.14.2 helper.h int add(int a, int b); 5.14.3 helper.c int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; } Build with: gcc main.c helper.c -o program Now the interface and implementation are both included. If you forget helper.c, you may get a link-time error. That is a valuable teaching moment. 5.15 Header files as interfaces Headers (.h) are commonly used to declare what functions are available. They do not usually hold the full implementation. At a beginner level, the main takeaway is: Headers tell the compiler what to expect. Source files provide the actual code. This makes larger projects more organized and easier to compile piece by piece. 5.16 Optimization flags A common practical compile command is: gcc hello.c -o hello -O2 Useful optimization levels to know: -O0 = no optimization -O2 = common practical default -O3 = more aggressive optimization For an introductory course, -O2 is a reasonable example because it introduces the idea that the build process can affect performance. The important conceptual point is not to memorize every flag. It is to realize that how a program is compiled can matter. 5.17 Warnings are not noise Use: gcc -Wall hello.c -o hello -Wall asks the compiler to show a wider class of warnings. Warnings do not always stop the build, but they are often useful clues. Strong beginners learn to read them early instead of ignoring them. A good principle is: If the compiler is trying to tell you something, pay attention before the project becomes larger. 5.18 A brief note on C++ A similar pattern exists for C++: g++ hello.cpp -o hello_cpp The overall workflow remains familiar: write source invoke compiler produce executable run executable This chapter uses C because it gives the cleanest first exposure to the build process. 5.19 Makefiles: why build automation exists As projects grow, repeated build commands become tiresome and error-prone. A Makefile automates the process. Create: nano Makefile Add: all: gcc -Wall -O2 hello.c -o hello Then run: make This is not just a convenience trick. It teaches a valuable lesson: Build steps are part of the workflow and should be made repeatable. 5.20 A more realistic Makefile example CC=gcc CFLAGS=-Wall -O2 all: program program: main.o helper.o $(CC) $(CFLAGS) main.o helper.o -o program main.o: main.c helper.h $(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c main.c helper.o: helper.c helper.h $(CC) $(CFLAGS) -c helper.c clean: rm -f *.o program Even if you are not yet comfortable writing Makefiles from scratch, reading one helps you understand how real projects are assembled. 5.21 Where to compile For small builds, compile on the login node. That is appropriate because compilation is often part of setup work. However, very large builds or special workflows may require different strategies depending on system policy and project needs. For this course, the safe beginner pattern is: edit on the login node compile on the login node run real jobs on compute nodes via SLURM 5.22 Running compiled code through SLURM Once the executable exists, a batch script can run it. Example: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J c_hello #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o c_hello_%j.out #SBATCH -e c_hello_%j.err ./hello Submit with: sbatch run_hello.slurm This connects Chapter 4 directly to Chapter 3: building and scheduling are different phases of the workflow. 5.23 A practical build-and-run pattern A typical beginner workflow might look like this: module load gcc gcc -Wall -O2 hello.c -o hello ls -l hello ./hello sbatch run_hello.slurm This order is instructional gold because it separates concerns clearly: load environment build executable test executable lightly run real work through the scheduler 5.24 Common compiler and build errors 5.24.1 Error type 1: syntax problem Symptoms: - missing semicolon - typo in a function name - unbalanced braces or parentheses Response: - read the line number - inspect nearby lines - fix syntax before trying other theories 5.24.2 Error type 2: missing header Symptoms: - compiler says it cannot find a header file Response: - check the include line - confirm the file exists - confirm search paths if using nonstandard locations 5.24.3 Error type 3: undefined reference Symptoms: - the compiler gets farther, then the linker complains Response: - check whether all needed .c files were included - check whether required libraries were linked 5.24.4 Error type 4: command not found Symptoms: - shell cannot find gcc Response: - check module load gcc - run which gcc 5.25 Failure case study 1: typo in source code Suppose you accidentally write: #include <stdio.h> int main() { printf("Hello
") return 0; } The missing semicolon after printf will prevent compilation. Lesson: - the executable cannot update until the source is valid 5.26 Failure case study 2: typo in the compile command Suppose you run: gcc hell.c -o hello but the file is actually named hello.c. Lesson: - sometimes the mistake is not in the source, but in the shell command itself Use: ls to confirm filenames rather than relying on memory. 5.27 Failure case study 3: object exists, executable is stale Suppose you edit hello.c, forget to rebuild, and run ./hello. You may see old behavior and think the edits failed. What really happened is simpler: source changed executable did not Lesson: - rebuilding is part of the development loop 5.28 Reading compiler messages calmly Compiler output can look intimidating because it is dense. A good beginner habit is to read it in layers: identify the first real error find the file and line number inspect the local code near that line fix one issue at a time recompile Do not scroll immediately to the bottom and panic. Often the first meaningful error is the most useful one. 5.29 Good habits for build workflows load the compiler module explicitly check which gcc and gcc --version keep source files organized rebuild after edits use -Wall automate repetitive builds with make keep run scripts separate from source files treat build commands as part of reproducible workflow documentation 5.30 Lab 1: build and run a first C program Create hello.c Load gcc Compile with gcc hello.c -o hello Run ./hello Confirm the executable exists with ls -l 5.31 Lab 2: use warnings Recompile using gcc -Wall hello.c -o hello Introduce a small issue Observe how warnings or errors change Fix the issue and recompile 5.32 Lab 3: build a multi-file program Create main.c, helper.c, and helper.h Compile them together Run the result Remove one source file from the compile command intentionally Observe and interpret the resulting error 5.33 Lab 4: automate with make Create a simple Makefile Build with make Edit the source Build again Explain why a Makefile is useful in repeatable workflows 5.34 Stretch goal Create a small program that reads input, performs a calculation, and writes output. Then: compile it with warnings enabled create a SLURM script to run it store source in src/ store the job script in jobs/ document the build and run commands in a README.md 5.35 Key takeaways source code and executables are different artifacts compiler modules are part of the HPC environment compile-time and link-time errors are different kinds of failures warnings are useful, not just noise Makefiles turn repeated build steps into reproducible workflow small programs can be built on the login node, but real execution belongs under SLURM
