Chapter 6 Parallel Computing with MPI and OpenMP on LS6 6.1 Overview Up to this point, the course has focused on a basic but essential HPC workflow: connect to the system work safely on the login node manage files and environment modules submit jobs through SLURM compile programs from source The next conceptual step is parallel computing. Parallel computing is one of the main reasons HPC systems exist. A cluster is valuable not only because it is larger than a laptop, but because it allows work to be spread across multiple cores, multiple processes, and sometimes multiple nodes. This chapter introduces the two most common beginner models: MPI for distributed-memory parallelism OpenMP for shared-memory parallelism By the end of the chapter, you should be able to: explain the difference between serial and parallel execution explain the difference between processes and threads distinguish tasks, cores, and threads in a practical way describe when MPI and OpenMP are appropriate compile a small MPI program compile a small OpenMP program connect SLURM resource requests to the program model being used 6.2 Why parallel computing matters A single CPU core can execute only one stream of instructions at a time. For small tasks, that may be enough. For larger tasks, it becomes a bottleneck. Parallel computing helps when: the work can be broken into pieces those pieces can be done at the same time the time to solution matters the dataset or simulation is large many repeated calculations must be carried out efficiently In practice, HPC workloads often take one of these forms: many independent jobs one program using multiple processes one program using multiple threads hybrid workflows combining both processes and threads One of the most important learning goals here is to stop treating “parallel” as one vague idea. There are different models, and the resource request should reflect the model. 6.3 Mental model: serial versus parallel A serial program performs one step after another: Task 1 -> Task 2 -> Task 3 -> Task 4 A parallel program attempts to perform multiple pieces of work concurrently: Task 1 Task 2 -> running at the same time Task 3 Task 4 This does not automatically guarantee a speedup. Parallelism introduces overhead, coordination, and sometimes communication cost. That is why the real question is not just: Can I use more cores? It is: Does this workload benefit from more cores, and which model fits it best? 6.4 Processes, threads, tasks, and cores These terms often get blurred together. It is better to separate them. 6.4.1 Core A hardware execution unit on a CPU. 6.4.2 Process An independent running program instance with its own memory space. 6.4.3 Thread A lighter-weight execution path within a process, usually sharing memory with other threads in that same process. 6.4.4 Task (in SLURM) A scheduler concept often used to represent one process slot or one program instance in parallel execution. For beginners, the practical takeaway is: MPI usually maps naturally to multiple processes/tasks OpenMP usually maps naturally to multiple threads on one process cores are the hardware resources underneath both models 6.5 Two main models 6.6 MPI: distributed-memory parallelism MPI stands for Message Passing Interface. Mental model: you run multiple processes each process has its own memory space processes communicate by sending messages This model is especially useful when work is distributed across multiple processes and possibly multiple nodes. 6.6.1 Why MPI matters MPI is a standard approach when: the program is written for distributed memory processes must cooperate across one or more nodes the workload scales beyond the memory or cores of a single process 6.6.2 Key idea MPI processes do not automatically share memory the way threads do. Communication must be explicit. 6.7 OpenMP: shared-memory parallelism OpenMP is a threading model. Mental model: one program starts that program creates multiple threads the threads share memory inside the same node This model is especially useful when: the program is running on one node shared-memory parallelism is sufficient the work can be split across loops or sections without needing message passing 6.8 A decision rule for beginners A useful first approximation is: use MPI when the workload is designed as multiple communicating processes use OpenMP when the workload is designed as multiple threads sharing memory on a node That is not the whole world of parallel computing, but it is a strong start. 6.9 Parallelism is not the same as “asking for more resources” A repeated beginner mistake is to request more tasks or cores without changing the program. If a plain serial program is run like this: python analysis.py then asking for four tasks does not magically make it parallel. Similarly, an OpenMP program may request four cores, but if OMP_NUM_THREADS is never set, the program may not use them as intended. The scheduler grants resources. It does not rewrite your code. 6.10 MPI hello world Load an MPI environment: module load openmpi Create hello_mpi.c: #include <mpi.h> #include <stdio.h> int main(int argc, char** argv) { MPI_Init(&argc, &argv); int rank, size; MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &rank); MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, &size); printf("Hello from process %d of %d\n", rank, size); MPI_Finalize(); return 0; } Compile with: mpicc hello_mpi.c -o hello_mpi mpicc is a compiler wrapper that helps build MPI programs with the appropriate MPI libraries. 6.11 A quick local-style test A short test might use: mpirun -np 4 ./hello_mpi However, on a shared HPC system, real runs should usually go through SLURM so that resource allocation remains explicit and appropriate. 6.12 MPI through SLURM Example script: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J mpi_hello #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 4 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o mpi_%j.out #SBATCH -e mpi_%j.err module load openmpi mpirun ./hello_mpi Interpretation: -N 1 requests one node -n 4 requests four tasks the MPI program runs as four cooperating processes This is a good place to reinforce the mental model: In an MPI job, tasks usually correspond to processes. 6.13 What should the output look like? You might see something like: Hello from process 0 of 4 Hello from process 1 of 4 Hello from process 2 of 4 Hello from process 3 of 4 The order may vary because parallel processes do not necessarily print in rank order. That in itself is an important lesson: parallel execution can produce nondeterministic ordering in output. 6.14 OpenMP hello world Create hello_omp.c: #include <stdio.h> #include <omp.h> int main() { #pragma omp parallel { int id = omp_get_thread_num(); int nthreads = omp_get_num_threads(); printf("Hello from thread %d of %d\n", id, nthreads); } return 0; } Compile with: gcc -fopenmp hello_omp.c -o hello_omp The -fopenmp flag enables OpenMP support. 6.15 Run the OpenMP program Set the number of threads: export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 ./hello_omp Expected pattern: Hello from thread 0 of 4 Hello from thread 1 of 4 Hello from thread 2 of 4 Hello from thread 3 of 4 Again, output order may vary. 6.16 OpenMP through SLURM Example script: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J omp_hello #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -c 4 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o omp_%j.out #SBATCH -e omp_%j.err export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 ./hello_omp Interpretation: -n 1 requests one task/process -c 4 requests four CPU cores for that task OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 tells the program to use four threads This is the key beginner OpenMP pattern: one process, multiple threads, one shared-memory node context 6.17 Tasks versus cores: why this confuses people This is where many beginners get mixed up. 6.17.1 MPI-like pattern several tasks usually one process per task communication among processes potentially across multiple nodes 6.17.2 OpenMP-like pattern one task several cores for that task one process creates multiple threads usually within one node If you remember only one practical distinction, make it this: MPI usually scales by adding processes. OpenMP usually scales by adding threads. 6.18 A side-by-side comparison 6.18.1 MPI script pattern #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 4 module load openmpi mpirun ./hello_mpi 6.18.2 OpenMP script pattern #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -c 4 export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 ./hello_omp The resource requests are different because the program models are different. 6.19 Hybrid thinking Some applications use both MPI and OpenMP. Mental model: Multiple MPI processes ↓ Each process creates multiple threads That can be useful, but it also makes the resource request more complex. For an introductory course, it is enough to understand the concept without requiring deep hybrid optimization. 6.20 Strong scaling and weak scaling Performance discussions often use two ideas. 6.20.1 Strong scaling Same problem size, more resources. Question: - does runtime decrease when cores increase? 6.20.2 Weak scaling Problem size grows as resources grow. Question: - can the workload size increase while runtime stays roughly manageable? These ideas matter because “faster with more cores” is not the whole story. Parallel performance depends on how the workload and resources interact. 6.21 Why speedup is not automatic Beginners often imagine that doubling cores should cut time in half. Real life is more complicated. Reasons speedup may fall short: communication overhead synchronization cost load imbalance memory bottlenecks serial parts of the code poor match between program and requested resources This is why performance thinking matters as much as syntax. 6.22 A simple timing habit Use: time ./hello or time the relevant production code in a more meaningful example. The key lesson is that parallel claims should be supported by evidence, not assumption. 6.23 Common beginner mistakes 6.23.1 Mistake 1: requesting four tasks for a serial program Correction: - the code remains serial unless it is written or launched in a parallel way 6.23.2 Mistake 2: confusing tasks with threads Correction: - tasks usually correspond to processes - threads are created within a process 6.23.3 Mistake 3: using OpenMP code without setting OMP_NUM_THREADS Correction: - thread count must usually be specified deliberately 6.23.4 Mistake 4: running MPI directly on the login node as a habit Correction: - small tests may be possible, but real runs belong under SLURM 6.23.5 Mistake 5: assuming output order reveals execution order cleanly Correction: - parallel output can interleave unpredictably 6.24 Failure case study 1: resource request does not match the MPI code Suppose an MPI program is launched with only one task, but you expect four processes to appear. Possible causes: - -n was set incorrectly - the launch command did not match the intended run - the program was built or run under the wrong assumptions Lesson: - in MPI workflows, process count is a real design parameter, not a decorative number 6.25 Failure case study 2: OpenMP script requests four cores but uses one thread Suppose the job script includes: #SBATCH -c 4 but never sets: export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 The job may use only one thread. Lesson: - the scheduler grants cores - the program still needs to be told how many threads to create 6.26 Failure case study 3: parallel job is slower than the serial version This often surprises people the first time they see it. Possible reasons: - the problem is too small - communication overhead dominates - startup overhead dominates - the implementation is inefficient - the resource request does not fit the code Lesson: - parallelism is a strategy, not a magic spell 6.27 Debugging checklist for parallel jobs When a parallel run looks wrong, ask: 6.27.1 1. What model am I using? serial MPI OpenMP hybrid 6.27.2 2. Does the compile step match the model? mpicc for MPI gcc -fopenmp for OpenMP 6.27.3 3. Does the SLURM request match the model? tasks for MPI cores/threads for OpenMP 6.27.4 4. Are the relevant environment variables set? especially OMP_NUM_THREADS 6.27.5 5. What does the output file actually show? process counts thread counts unexpected ordering crashes or missing output 6.27.6 6. Is the workload large enough to reveal meaningful parallel behavior? Small toy problems often hide or distort scaling. 6.28 Best practices decide first whether the code is serial, MPI, or OpenMP request resources that match the model make process and thread counts visible in output begin with small tests measure rather than assume speedup keep job scripts readable and explicit 6.29 Lab 1: MPI hello world Create hello_mpi.c Load the MPI environment Compile with mpicc Create a SLURM script requesting four tasks Submit the job Inspect the output and explain rank and size 6.30 Lab 2: OpenMP hello world Create hello_omp.c Compile with gcc -fopenmp Create a SLURM script with one task and four cores Set OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 Submit the job Inspect the output and explain thread count 6.31 Lab 3: compare the models conceptually Create a small written comparison answering: what is a process? what is a thread? how do the SLURM requests differ for MPI and OpenMP? when would you choose each model? 6.32 Lab 4: measure a tiny scaling experiment For a simple parallel program: run with 1, 2, and 4 processes or threads record runtimes explain whether the results are convincing or limited discuss why the speedup may not be perfectly linear 6.33 Stretch goal Create a small compute kernel that performs repeated arithmetic or loop work. Implement either: an MPI version using multiple ranks, or an OpenMP version using multiple threads Then write a short reflection on whether the parallel model actually helped. 6.34 Key takeaways parallel computing is about matching workload structure to resource use MPI uses multiple processes and explicit communication OpenMP uses multiple threads in shared memory tasks, cores, and threads are related but not interchangeable terms SLURM grants resources; it does not automatically parallelize your program speedup should be measured, not assumed

Chapter 7 Containers and Reproducible HPC Workflows 7.1 Overview One of the most common frustrations in computational work is this sentence: It worked on my machine. Containers are one of the main ways modern computing tries to solve that problem. In HPC, containers help package software environments so that workflows become more portable and more reproducible. They are not magic, and they do not remove the need to understand the host system, but they are powerful when used correctly. This chapter introduces containers as a practical HPC tool. The focus is not on buzzwords. The focus is on how containers fit into real LS6-style workflow. By the end of the chapter, you should be able to: explain what a container is explain why containers matter in HPC distinguish Docker from Apptainer at a practical level pull or use a simple Apptainer image execute commands inside a container understand why directory binding matters run a containerized command through SLURM recognize common beginner container mistakes 7.2 Mental model: what a container actually is A container packages: your program required libraries supporting software dependencies environment expectations This means a container image can act like a portable execution environment. A simple mental picture is: Your code + supporting software + libraries + environment assumptions -------------------------------- Container image The key idea is not that the container replaces the whole HPC system. It does not. Instead, it packages much of the user-space environment so that software behavior becomes more consistent across contexts. 7.3 Why containers matter in HPC Containers are useful because they can reduce or manage problems like: missing dependencies conflicting library versions “works on one machine but not another” behavior hard-to-reconstruct software setups brittle custom environments In research computing, this matters because reproducibility is often not just a convenience issue. It affects whether others can repeat your workflow months later. 7.4 Containers do not eliminate the host system A very important correction: A container is not a full replacement for the HPC system around it. You still depend on: the host kernel the scheduler the filesystem cluster policies the available hardware the way input and output are connected to the environment So the right mental model is: the host system provides infrastructure the container provides a packaged software environment Both matter. 7.5 Docker versus Apptainer 7.5.1 Docker Docker is widely used in development and deployment environments. It is a major tool in software engineering and cloud workflows. However, on shared HPC systems, Docker often requires privileges or daemon-based behavior that are not a good fit for multi-user clusters. 7.5.2 Apptainer Apptainer is designed for HPC-style use. Practical advantages: better suited to shared environments commonly used without root privileges during normal execution integrates naturally into scheduler-based workflows fits better with cluster security models For this course, the practical message is simple: Docker is part of the broader container world, but Apptainer is the HPC-friendly execution model you are more likely to encounter directly on systems like LS6. 7.6 Checking for Apptainer First check whether it is already available: which apptainer If needed, load it as a module: module load apptainer Then verify again: which apptainer This mirrors earlier course logic: software availability on HPC systems is often environment-dependent. 7.7 Pulling a simple image Example: apptainer pull ubuntu.sif docker://ubuntu Interpretation: docker://ubuntu identifies the source image ubuntu.sif is the output image file .sif is a common Apptainer image format This command makes the image a tangible file in your working environment. 7.8 Run a command inside the container Use: apptainer exec ubuntu.sif echo "Hello from container" This is the cleanest way to understand container execution. You are not “booting a virtual machine.” You are asking Apptainer to run a command inside the packaged environment represented by the image. 7.9 Use an interactive shell apptainer shell ubuntu.sif This opens an interactive shell inside the container environment. Useful exploratory checks include: which python3 python3 --version ls / This helps you see that the container has its own environment logic while still running on the host. 7.10 Binding directories: the most important beginner concept One of the most common container confusions is visibility of files. A container image does not automatically expose every host path the way you might expect. Often, you need to bind directories explicitly. Example: apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif ls This makes the current working directory visible inside the container for the command being run. If your workflow depends on scripts, inputs, or outputs in the current directory, binding is often essential. 7.11 Why binding matters Without an appropriate bind, a containerized command may not see: your script your input data your working directory your intended output location That often produces a confusing symptom: The container starts fine, but it cannot find my files. Usually the container is not “broken.” The host paths simply were not connected the way you assumed. 7.12 A concrete example with a host script Suppose script.py exists in the current directory. You might try: apptainer exec ubuntu.sif python3 script.py If the container does not see that host directory, the script may not be found. A better pattern is: apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif python3 script.py This makes the relationship between host files and the container explicit. 7.13 Store images in sensible places Container images can be large. Good storage decisions matter. Practical advice: use $WORK or another project-oriented location for sizable images avoid cluttering small quota-limited areas like $HOME with large images think of images as data assets that should be placed deliberately This connects containers back to the filesystem lessons earlier in the course. 7.14 Running Python inside a container Example: apptainer exec ubuntu.sif python3 --version This is useful because it shows how containers can provide software versions that may not match the default host environment. 7.15 Containerizing a workflow through SLURM A batch script can run a containerized command just as it runs other programs. Example: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J container_test #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o container_%j.out #SBATCH -e container_%j.err module load apptainer apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif echo "Running inside container" Submit with: sbatch container.slurm This is an important conceptual bridge: Containers do not replace SLURM. Containers are often executed under SLURM as part of a scheduled workflow. 7.16 A more realistic containerized script run Suppose you have a Python script in the current working directory: #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J container_python #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:10:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o container_py_%j.out #SBATCH -e container_py_%j.err module load apptainer apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif python3 script.py This is a realistic pattern for teaching because it combines: scheduler awareness software environment packaging explicit path handling reproducible command execution 7.17 When containers help most Containers are especially useful when: software dependencies are awkward collaborators need the same environment the host system and project software differ significantly reproducibility matters over time modules alone are not enough or become cumbersome 7.18 When modules may be enough Not every workflow needs a container. Sometimes the module system already gives you: a suitable compiler the right Python stack the right MPI stack the needed application version In those cases, using modules alone may be simpler. This is an important judgment skill: Use the tool that fits the workflow. Do not use containers just because they sound modern. 7.19 Common beginner mistakes 7.19.1 Mistake 1: assuming the container sees all host files automatically Correction: - bind the directories you actually need 7.19.2 Mistake 2: forgetting to load Apptainer Correction: - verify availability with which apptainer - load the module if needed 7.19.3 Mistake 3: storing large images in the wrong location Correction: - choose storage locations intentionally 7.19.4 Mistake 4: thinking a container replaces SLURM Correction: - scheduled execution still goes through SLURM on shared systems 7.19.5 Mistake 5: assuming every needed tool is already inside the image Correction: - inspect the image and verify commands rather than assuming 7.20 Failure case study 1: file not found inside the container Symptom: - the script exists on the host - containerized execution says the file is missing Likely cause: - the working directory was not bound into the container Fix: - add --bind $PWD or another appropriate bind path 7.21 Failure case study 2: command not found inside the image Symptom: - python3 or another expected command is missing Likely cause: - the image does not actually contain that software Fix: - inspect the image interactively - choose a more appropriate image - build or pull a container that matches the workflow 7.22 Failure case study 3: output appears in the wrong place Symptom: - the job runs, but expected output is not visible where you expect it Likely causes: - output path inside the container does not map to the host path - bind paths were incomplete or misunderstood Fix: - make host/container path relationships explicit - write outputs to bound directories intentionally 7.23 Debugging checklist for container workflows When a containerized job behaves strangely, ask: 7.23.1 1. Is Apptainer available? which apptainer 7.23.2 2. Does the image exist? ls *.sif 7.23.3 3. Can I explore it interactively? apptainer shell ubuntu.sif 7.23.4 4. Does the command I need actually exist inside the image? apptainer exec ubuntu.sif which python3 7.23.5 5. Are the needed host directories bound into the container? Check the --bind logic in the command or script. 7.23.6 6. Does the batch script still make sense as a SLURM job? Remember that the scheduler and the container are separate layers. 7.24 Best practices load Apptainer explicitly when needed inspect images rather than assuming contents bind host directories intentionally store images in appropriate storage areas keep container commands inside readable SLURM scripts document the image source and runtime assumptions in the project README 7.25 Lab 1: pull and inspect an image Load Apptainer if needed Pull a simple image Run apptainer exec with a trivial command Enter the image with apptainer shell Inspect software inside the image 7.26 Lab 2: bind the working directory Create a small host file Run a container command without a bind Observe what the container can or cannot see Repeat with --bind $PWD Explain the difference 7.27 Lab 3: run a containerized batch job Write a simple container.slurm Load Apptainer in the script Execute a containerized command Submit the job Inspect output and error files 7.28 Lab 4: compare modules and containers Write a short reflection answering: when are modules sufficient? when does a container help? what new complexity does a container introduce? 7.29 Stretch goal Run a small script through Apptainer under SLURM, with explicit bind paths and recorded output. Then write a short note explaining how another user could reproduce the workflow from scratch. 7.30 Key takeaways containers package software environments, not the entire HPC system Apptainer is the HPC-friendly container tool in this course context directory binding is one of the most important practical concepts containers often run under SLURM, not instead of SLURM modules and containers solve different workflow problems reproducibility improves when container usage is documented clearly

Chapter 8 Capstone Project: Building an End-to-End HPC Workflow 8.1 Overview The capstone project is where the course stops being a list of isolated skills and becomes a coherent workflow. Earlier chapters asked you to learn pieces: navigate LS6 through the shell work deliberately in the filesystem manage environment modules write and submit SLURM jobs inspect output and debug failures compile software run parallel programs use containers for reproducible execution The capstone asks you to assemble those pieces into one documented project that another person could understand and repeat. This chapter explains: what a good capstone project looks like how large or small it should be how to structure the project directory what deliverables should be included how to demonstrate reproducibility and resource awareness how to write a useful debugging reflection 8.2 The philosophy of the capstone A beginner sometimes thinks the goal of HPC is simply to “make code run.” That is not enough. A strong HPC workflow should also be: understandable repeatable resource-aware debuggable explainable to another user This is why the capstone is not only about output. It is about workflow maturity. You are showing that you can move from: “I got something to work somehow” to: “I understand the environment, the build process, the scheduler request, the output, and the choices I made.” 8.3 What counts as a successful capstone A successful capstone does not need to be huge. A modest project can be excellent if it is: organized clearly run correctly through SLURM documented honestly accompanied by output and evidence explicit about what was learned A small project with strong workflow discipline is often much better than a giant project held together by copy-paste and guesswork. 8.4 What the capstone should demonstrate At minimum, a capstone should show that you can: organize a project directory on LS6 prepare code or scripts intentionally load the needed environment submit at least one real job capture output and inspect it explain the resources requested diagnose or reflect on at least one issue encountered write enough documentation that another person could follow the workflow That last point matters. In research computing, documentation is part of the work. 8.5 Four capstone styles The course supports several project styles. Choose one that fits the skills you want to emphasize. 8.6 Option 1: compile and run a C or C++ program This is a good fit if you want to emphasize: compiler modules source files and executables Makefiles or build commands job submission of compiled code 8.6.1 Minimum ingredients source code compile command or Makefile SLURM job script output file README describing the workflow 8.6.2 Strong additions warnings enabled in the build small input/output example note about why the resource request is appropriate 8.7 Option 2: parallel program This is a good fit if you want to emphasize: MPI or OpenMP tasks versus threads matching resource requests to program structure basic scaling or comparison runs 8.7.1 Minimum ingredients parallel source code compile step SLURM job script output from at least one successful run reflection on the parallel model used 8.7.2 Strong additions compare 1 versus 2 versus 4 tasks or threads explain whether the result was convincing discuss overhead or scaling limitations 8.8 Option 3: containerized workflow This is a good fit if you want to emphasize: reproducibility environment portability Apptainer execution explicit bind-path thinking 8.8.1 Minimum ingredients container image or source description script or workflow executed inside the container SLURM job script explanation of bind paths output from a successful run 8.8.2 Strong additions comparison to a module-only workflow note about why the container was useful clear documentation of image source and assumptions 8.9 Option 4: hybrid workflow This option combines course ideas. Examples: - compiled MPI program run through SLURM - compiled program executed in a container - serial and parallel versions of the same program - workflow with build, execution, and post-run inspection documented end to end This option is useful if it genuinely fits your project, but it should not become complicated just for show. 8.10 Project scale: how big is “big enough”? A common worry is that the project must be impressive in scope. That is not the right goal. A capstone is big enough when it: demonstrates real HPC workflow includes documented evidence shows conscious resource use contains enough moving parts to reveal understanding A one-file serial program with a careful SLURM script and excellent documentation can be a better capstone than an overambitious project that no one can reproduce. 8.11 A recommended directory structure A clean project structure helps the reader understand the workflow. Example: project/ ├── src/ ├── scripts/ ├── jobs/ ├── output/ ├── env/ └── README.md Suggested meanings: src/ for source code scripts/ for helper scripts jobs/ for SLURM submission files output/ for job output and result files env/ for notes about modules, build steps, or container images README.md for project explanation The exact names can vary, but the project should communicate structure clearly. 8.12 What belongs in the README The README is one of the most important deliverables. At minimum, it should answer: What does this project do? What files matter most? What software environment is required? How do you build it? How do you run it? What output should the user expect? What did you learn or debug along the way? A README turns your project from a private memory into a reproducible artifact. 8.13 Reproducibility: the real standard A project is reproducible when another person could follow the documented steps and obtain the same workflow behavior. That means avoiding hidden assumptions such as: “just run this from the right place” “use whatever module works” “it should already be installed” “I forgot exactly how I built it” Reproducibility is improved when you write down: module loads build commands job submission commands expected outputs known limitations 8.14 Resource awareness is part of the grade The capstone should show that you understand the scheduler request, not just the program. You should be able to explain: why you requested a certain number of nodes or tasks why the wall time is reasonable whether the workload is serial, threaded, or process-based whether the request fits the program A capstone that asks for four tasks and runs a plain serial job should be treated as incomplete understanding, even if the job technically runs. 8.15 Debugging reflection is required Almost every real computational workflow includes some kind of failure, revision, or adjustment. That is normal. Your project should include at least one concise debugging reflection, such as: a missing module load caused command failure a compile error came from a typo the job was submitted from the wrong directory an MPI task count did not match the expected process count a container could not see the intended files until a bind path was added The goal is not to pretend nothing went wrong. The goal is to show that you can diagnose and respond intelligently. 8.16 Example capstone template 1: compiled serial program 8.16.1 Files src/hello.c jobs/run_hello.slurm README.md output/slurm-123456.out 8.16.2 Workflow load gcc compile hello.c run ./hello as a short test submit jobs/run_hello.slurm inspect the output file document the steps in README.md 8.16.3 What this demonstrates compilation executable management SLURM submission output inspection reproducibility basics 8.17 Example capstone template 2: OpenMP program 8.17.1 Files src/hello_omp.c jobs/run_omp.slurm README.md output/omp_123456.out 8.17.2 Workflow compile with gcc -fopenmp request one task and multiple cores set OMP_NUM_THREADS run through SLURM inspect output showing thread IDs explain why -n 1 and -c 4 make sense 8.17.3 What this demonstrates thread-based parallelism scheduler-resource alignment explanation of model choice 8.18 Example capstone template 3: containerized Python script 8.18.1 Files scripts/script.py jobs/run_container.slurm env/container_notes.txt README.md output/container_py_123456.out 8.18.2 Workflow identify or pull an image load Apptainer bind the working directory run script.py through the container in a SLURM job document the image source and bind assumptions 8.18.3 What this demonstrates reproducible environment thinking scheduler plus container interaction path-awareness 8.19 How Your Work Will Be Evaluated 8.19.1 Workflow correctness (30%) does the build/run path work? does the SLURM job complete? is output present? 8.19.2 Documentation quality (25%) is the README clear? are commands and files explained? can a reader follow the workflow? 8.19.3 Reproducibility (20%) are environment assumptions explicit? are steps repeatable? are hidden dependencies minimized? 8.19.4 Resource awareness (15%) do the scheduler requests make sense? do you show that you understand tasks, threads, and wall time? 8.19.5 Debugging reflection (10%) is there an honest description of a problem and how it was fixed? This evaluation rewards the habits the course is trying to build. 8.20 Common capstone mistakes 8.20.1 Mistake 1: no README Without documentation, the project becomes difficult to interpret or reproduce. 8.20.2 Mistake 2: output exists, but no explanation A result file without workflow context is incomplete. 8.20.3 Mistake 3: wrong resource request The project may run, but the request reveals misunderstanding. 8.20.4 Mistake 4: hidden assumptions If the project depends on unstated directory locations, modules, or files, reproducibility suffers. 8.20.5 Mistake 5: debugging omitted Real workflows involve corrections. Omitting them often hides the most educational part of the work. 8.21 How to write the debugging reflection A good debugging reflection is short and specific. Useful structure: Problem What went wrong? Evidence What output, error file, or observation revealed it? Diagnosis What caused the issue? Fix What did you change? Lesson learned What will you do differently next time? Example: The initial job failed because the script assumed the executable was in the current directory, but I had submitted the job from a different location. I noticed the error in the output file when the executable could not be found. I fixed the issue by submitting from the project root and using a clearer path. The lesson was that working directory assumptions should be made explicit. 8.22 Final submission checklist Before turning in a capstone, ask: does the project directory make sense? are the important files clearly named? can I explain the resource request? do the output files support my claims? have I documented build and run steps? have I included at least one debugging reflection? could another user reproduce this? 8.23 Lab: draft your capstone plan Before building the final project, write a one-page project plan answering: which capstone option will I choose? what files will I need? what environment will I load? how will I run the job? what output will I collect? what resource request makes sense? what might go wrong? This planning exercise often prevents weak project structure later. 8.24 Stretch goal Add a second run or variation that compares: serial versus parallel containerized versus module-based one task versus several tasks one optimization level versus another Then discuss what changed and why. 8.25 Key takeaways the capstone is a workflow demonstration, not just an output demonstration smaller, clearer, more reproducible projects are often better than overambitious ones README quality is a major part of project quality resource awareness and debugging reflection are essential a successful capstone shows that you understand the full path from environment setup to output interpretation

Chapter 9 Appendix: Quick Reference and Troubleshooting for LS6 Workflows 9.1 Overview This appendix is a compact reference for the commands, patterns, and reminders used throughout the course. It is not a replacement for the explanatory chapters. Instead, it is a quick recovery tool: when you already understand the ideas, this appendix helps you remember the exact command pattern or troubleshooting sequence. 9.2 Core workflow picture Keep this mental model in view: Laptop -> Login node -> SLURM -> Compute node(s) -> Output And keep this rule visible: Do setup on the login node. Do serious computation on compute nodes. 9.3 A safe beginner shell pattern Before doing something important, run: pwd ls This quickly confirms: where you are what files are present whether you are about to act in the intended directory That tiny habit prevents many avoidable mistakes. 9.4 SSH Connect to LS6: ssh your_username@ls6.tacc.utexas.edu 9.5 Filesystem basics Check current directory: pwd List files: ls ls -l Change directories: cd cd .. cd my_directory Create directories: mkdir project mkdir -p project/src project/jobs project/output Copy, move, rename, remove: cp oldfile newfile mv oldname newname rm file 9.6 Editing files Use a simple editor: nano myfile.txt 9.7 Permissions Make a script executable: chmod +x script.sh Inspect permissions: ls -l script.sh A common symptom of permission trouble is: the file exists but it will not run 9.8 Environment variables View the environment: env Check common variables: echo $HOME echo $PATH echo $WORK echo $SCRATCH Temporarily add a directory to PATH: export PATH=$HOME/bin:$PATH 9.9 Modules List available modules: module avail Load a module: module load gcc Inspect loaded modules: module list Unload a module: module unload gcc Check where a command comes from: which gcc 9.10 Compilers and build commands Basic C compile: gcc hello.c -o hello Compile with warnings and optimization: gcc -Wall -O2 hello.c -o hello Basic C++ compile: g++ hello.cpp -o hello_cpp Compile an OpenMP program: gcc -fopenmp hello_omp.c -o hello_omp Compile an MPI program: mpicc hello_mpi.c -o hello_mpi Build with make: make Clean with make clean if the Makefile provides it. 9.11 SLURM basics Submit a job: sbatch job.slurm Monitor current jobs: squeue -u $USER Inspect job history: sacct Inspect one job: sacct -j 123456 Cancel a job: scancel 123456 9.12 Common job states PD = pending R = running CG = completing CD = completed Remember: PD does not automatically mean broken sbatch does not mean “running right now” 9.13 Minimal serial batch script template #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J my_job #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o my_job_%j.out #SBATCH -e my_job_%j.err pwd hostname date 9.14 Minimal MPI batch script template #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J mpi_job #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 4 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o mpi_%j.out #SBATCH -e mpi_%j.err module load openmpi mpirun ./hello_mpi 9.15 Minimal OpenMP batch script template #!/bin/bash #SBATCH -J omp_job #SBATCH -N 1 #SBATCH -n 1 #SBATCH -c 4 #SBATCH -t 00:05:00 #SBATCH -p normal #SBATCH -o omp_%j.out #SBATCH -e omp_%j.err export OMP_NUM_THREADS=4 ./hello_omp 9.16 Output files Inspect output: cat slurm-*.out Inspect a custom output file: cat my_job_123456.out cat my_job_123456.err Use less when output is long: less my_job_123456.out 9.17 Container commands Check Apptainer: which apptainer Load it if necessary: module load apptainer Pull an image: apptainer pull ubuntu.sif docker://ubuntu Execute a command inside the image: apptainer exec ubuntu.sif echo "hello" Start an interactive shell: apptainer shell ubuntu.sif Bind the working directory: apptainer exec --bind $PWD ubuntu.sif ls 9.18 Quick mental model table Situation Good first question command does not run Is the right module loaded? job fails What do the output and error files say? build fails What is the first real compiler error? MPI output looks wrong Does the task count match the program model? OpenMP uses one thread Did I set OMP_NUM_THREADS? container cannot find files Did I bind the right host directory? 9.19 Troubleshooting checklist: shell and files Use when something feels wrong immediately at the command line. pwd ls which command_name module list Questions: - am I in the right directory? - is the file really present? - is the command actually available? - is the right environment loaded? 9.20 Troubleshooting checklist: build failure When compilation fails: read the first meaningful error note the file and line number inspect nearby source lines fix one issue at a time recompile Helpful commands: ls gcc -Wall hello.c -o hello make Useful questions: - is the file name correct? - did I forget a semicolon or brace? - did I forget a source file in the build? - did I load the compiler module? 9.21 Troubleshooting checklist: SLURM job failure When a job fails or produces no useful result: check whether it is still in the queue inspect job accounting read output and error files verify current directory and input files check software environment assumptions Helpful commands: squeue -u $USER sacct -j 123456 pwd ls cat my_job_123456.out cat my_job_123456.err Useful questions: - did the job actually start? - did the program inside the job fail? - am I using the right path? - is the right module loaded in the script? 9.22 Troubleshooting checklist: MPI and OpenMP For MPI: - did I compile with mpicc? - did I request the intended number of tasks? - does the launch command match the code? For OpenMP: - did I compile with -fopenmp? - did I request enough cores? - did I set OMP_NUM_THREADS? 9.23 Troubleshooting checklist: containers When container behavior is confusing: which apptainer ls *.sif apptainer exec image.sif which python3 apptainer exec --bind $PWD image.sif ls Useful questions: - is Apptainer available? - does the image exist? - does the image contain the tool I need? - did I bind the directory containing my files? 9.24 Course workflow checklist Before running a real job, confirm: I am in the intended directory the needed files exist the required module(s) are loaded the build step completed successfully the SLURM request matches the workload the output path is clear I know what success should look like 9.25 Capstone checklist Before submitting the capstone project, confirm: the project directory is organized the README explains build and run steps the output files support the claims the resource request is justified at least one debugging reflection is included another user could reproduce the workflow 9.26 Final reminder Use the explanatory chapters to understand the ideas. Use this appendix when you already know the idea and need the command pattern, checklist, or first troubleshooting step quickly.
